data.title: Amprius Technologies: Growth Continues To Impress, But Valuation Is Getting Lofty
data.description:
I'm providing an update for my coverage on Amprius Technologies (AMPX). The company reported Q4 2025 results and had some additional developments since my last article. Amprius is achieving strong revenue growth along with margin expansion. The margin gains are driving the company towards profitability. While I love the growth that Amprius is achieving, I still have valuation concerns.

The company background information can be read in my first Amprius article.

AMPX's battery technology received the Best of Innovation award at CES for its silicon anode lithium-ion batteries. Amprius was chosen over thousands of entrants. The deciding factor came down to AMPX's batteries providing 520 watt-hours per kilogram. That is 2x the energy density of conventional graphite-based lithium-ion cells. This high-energy density enables the batteries to perform much better in their key applications.

Growth Catalysts

Amprius finished 2025 with strength as the company grew revenue by 137% to $25.2 million, beating estimates by $2.33 million in Q4. This helped drive the 3x gain in revenue to $73 million for the full year. These strong top-line results demonstrate the demand for AMPX's high-density silicon anode lithium-ion batteries. The company's batteries have 2x the energy density, range, and flight time as compared to conventional graphite-based battery cells. This advantage helps drive the demand for the use of AMPX's batteries for drone usage.

Amprius has also been achieving strong margin increases. The gross margin increased from a negative (21%) in Q4 2024 up to a positive 23.7% in Q4 2025. The gross margin increased from a negative (76%) for the full year of 2024 up to a positive 11.3% in 2025.

The operating margin is still negative. However, the margin improvements that Amprius is making are getting the company closer to profitability. The operating margin did improve from a negative (184%) in 2024 to a negative (33%) in 2025. Amprius may get close to profitability in 2026 but will more likely achieve it in 2027. AMPX is expecting a net loss of less than $8 million, or $0.06 per share, in 2026.

Amprius gained a major customer for unmanned aerial vehicles [UAVs]. The company picked up Nokia Drone Networks as a customer. AMPX's batteries provide Nokia drones with sufficient burst power for takeoff with sustained energy for extended flights along with safe returns/landings. AMPX's technology enables drones to fly longer, to carry more, and to operate in challenging conditions. This helps customers to reduce downtime and increase safety, which increases the overall value.

Amprius is ahead of its efforts to become NDAA compliant. This involves meeting the U.S. National Defense Authorization Act requirements for batteries. The compliance requires batteries to be assembled by a non-foreign entity of concern [FEOC]. So, that means to meet the requirement, the batteries must be assembled in the United States or in an allied nation.

Also, the functional cell components must be sourced from a non-FEOC. Compliance with this should help Amprius to obtain new contracts and/or extensions of existing contracts from the U.S. government. AMPX has until January 2028 to achieve full compliance. AMPX has three South Korean contract manufacturing partners and added a U.S.-based partner with Nanotech in California. An example of a U.S.-based award was a recent increase of a Department of War Defense Innovation Unit award in July 2025, which was increased to $14.8 million. This award is for Amprius to accelerate production of NDAA-compliant SiCore pouch cells used in military unmanned autonomous systems.

AMPX secured a $21 million purchase order from a new electric mobility customer in China for its SiCore cylindrical cells. Amprius' cells are to be used in this customer's light electric vehicles, which include scooters, motorcycles, and 3-wheelers. AMPX's SiCore cells were chosen for their high-energy density and long cycle life.

Amprius obtains about 75% of its revenue from outside of the U.S. That is a result of the specific markets that AMPX serves. For example, AMPX cells are used in Ukraine for drones that fly above 20,000 feet while carrying devices such as radar, LiDAR, cameras, a gimbal, communications equipment, and a secure flight controller. That adds a lot of weight to the drone, which creates the need for batteries that can handle the load. That's where AMPX's high-energy-density cells serve that need. Also, about 75% of AMPX's revenue comes from the defense market for these UAVs.

The light EV market comprises approximately the other 25% of AMPX's revenue. This market tends to be concentrated in China and other parts of Asia. However, the company has been gathering momentum in Europe for high-powered motorcycles, power tools, and similar products.

So, this leaves growth opportunity in the United States. Amprius anticipates that the U.S. will play catch-up for its products. AMPX sees the space market as a potential growth opportunity, where standard lithium-ion batteries fall short of handling that market's needs (i.e., backup power for antennas & receivers).

AMPX sees growth potential for electric vertical takeoff and landing [eVTOL] vehicles. These eVTOLs demand the high-powered cells that Amprius provides to launch effectively. One positive aspect of this application for AMPX is that it has more replacement needs as compared to EVs or stationary storage.

Amprius is estimating 2026 revenue to be $125 million. For the longer term, AMPX is targeting to achieve revenue of $600 million by 2030 with 30% gross margins and 20% EBITDA margins. The $600 million target is based on a total addressable market [TAM] of $2 billion just for batteries for the UAV space.

Valuation

The market tends to award Amprius with a premium valuation. It is not clear whether or not that will be sustainable over the long term. AMPX is trading with a forward price/sales ratio of 23.6x. This is significantly higher than the sector median forward price/sales of 1.9x. This is much higher than the forward price/sales of 8.5x when I wrote my November 2025 article.

Amprius does have strong long-term growth potential. So, I understand the market's enthusiasm for AMPX. However, I can't be confident that the market will just keep bidding the valuation up higher from here. I think the current valuation level puts the stock at risk for a steep correction on any unexpected disappointing news. That could include missing analysts' quarterly estimates or some other negative news.

Technical Analysis

AMPX's daily chart shows a bearish divergence between the stock price and the RSI from mid-March 2026 to the present. This occurs when the price makes a higher high, while the RSI indicator makes a lower high. Bearish divergences like this typically result in pullbacks from what I have observed. We can see in the chart that the stock also had a bearish divergence from September to October 2024. This resulted in the stock dropping from about $16 to just below $8.

The RSI is technically still bullish as it remains above the 50 level. A move below 50 would confirm a bearish trend. The MACD is also still technically bullish, as the blue MACD line remains above the red signal line. If the blue line crosses below the red signal line while the histogram bars turn red, it would also confirm a new bearish trend.

AMPX's next earnings report, scheduled for May 7, 2026, for Q1 2026 results, is the next likely catalyst. Analysts are expecting Q1 revenue to be $25.72 million and normalized EPS of -$0.02. AMPX's positive revenue momentum should allow the company to meet/exceed these estimates. Any news of new contract awards could be another positive catalyst for the stock.

Risks for the Stock

Competition could be a risk for Amprius. The company competes with Solid Power (SLDP), which makes sulfide-based batteries for EVs. QuantumScape (QS) is another competitor that produces lithium-metal batteries for EVs. SES AI (SES) produces lithium-metal and lithium-ion batteries for EVs, robotics, drones, etc. These competitors could take market share away from Amprius if their technology were proven to be more effective or desirable. It is also possible that these competitors increase their market share in the drone market, where Amprius has been thriving.

Since Amprius is still unprofitable, the company may have a need to issue more stock to continue to fund operations. AMPX issued $71 million in common stock in 2025. This was 50% higher than the $47.9 million of common stock that was issued in 2024. These equity raises can dilute shareholders as more shares enter the market. A large equity raise has the potential to trigger a sell-off for the stock.

Amprius Technologies Long-Term Outlook

The revenue growth that Amprius has been achieving continues to impress me. This helped drive strong margin expansion over the past year. Continued strong revenue gains and margin expansion should get AMPX to reach profitability approximately in 2027.

My concern remains that the valuation is lofty. The market may continue to give Amprius a premium valuation for its above-average revenue growth. However, I'm not comfortable with the current high forward price/sales ratio. The technical setup also points to a near-term possible correction for the stock. As a result of this, I will maintain my rating at 'HOLD' for AMPX.

data.title: I'm Not Ready To Write A Check For Visa, Earnings Preview
data.description:
With a market capitalization as of this writing of $573.3 billion, Visa (V) is more than a giant in the payment processing industry. It is one of the largest publicly traded companies on the planet. And yet, even though it commands a market-leading position and has done incredibly well as of late, its share price has lagged in the market. Since I last reaffirmed it as a ‘hold’ candidate back in January of this year, the stock has fallen 5.1%. This is at a time when the S&P 500 is pretty much flat. Although this is disappointing, it's not shocking to me. As I detailed in that article, even though it is valued at multiples that are consistent with other similar firms, it is absolutely expensive on an absolute basis. Not so expensive as to justify a bearish assessment. But expensive enough, even when factoring in its quality and market-leading position, to warrant more of a neutral stance than a bullish one.

I hope that, eventually, the company will justify an upgrade. After all, I think it's a fascinating business that is doing an exceptional job. The good news is that there will be an opportunity for new data to paint a more bullish picture for the business when management announces financial results for the second quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Those results should come out on April 28th. Leading up to that point, analysts believe that revenue and profitability will rise nicely. But unless something really amazing happens, I think that sticking with my neutral stance on it is justified for now.

I’m Not Ready to Upgrade Visa Yet

As of this writing, the only new data that investors have regarding Visa since I last wrote about the company covers through the first quarter of its 2026 fiscal year. During that time, revenue amounted to $10.90 billion. That's up nicely from the $9.51 billion that the business reported a year earlier. Management mentioned that this rise in revenue was driven mostly by growth associated with nominal cross-border volume, processed transactions, and nominal payments volume. The picture would have been better had it not been for the fact that the firm offered higher client incentives. Overall nominal payments volume for the business came in quite strong. For the US, we saw a rise of 7.1%, from $1.80 trillion to $1.93 trillion. And in international markets, the increase was 9.4%, from $2.24 trillion to $2.45 trillion.

Management has succeeded, as revenue has increased, to boost profitability also. Net profits surged from $5.12 billion to $5.85 billion. We saw a rise in operating cash flow from $5.40 billion to $6.78 billion. If we strip out changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $9.56 billion to $10.27 billion. And finally, EBITDA for the business expanded from $6.81 billion to $7.71 billion. This increase on the bottom line is part of a larger trend for the company. Over the last several years, management has succeeded in growing profitability as revenue increases. What really stands out to me, however, is just how dominant the business remains in this industry.

Take, if you will, the number of cards outstanding. That number was about 5 billion at the end of the quarter. That's up from the 4.7 billion cards reported at the same time a year earlier. This is not to say that other players in the space did not see an increase in cards also. Mastercard (MA) saw a rise from 3.1 billion to 3.4 billion. And American Express (AXP) enjoyed an increase from 146.5 million to 152.8 million. Even so, between these three enterprises, Visa boasts a market share of 58.52%. That is admittedly below the 58.95% that it had a year earlier. But still, that is significant and cements the company as the leader in its space.

Volumes are also worth taking a look at. As I mentioned already, the company benefited from a rise in overall volumes. Obviously, other players in the space did as well. For the most recent quarter, Mastercard saw an increase year over year amounting to 11%, while American Express saw an increase of 8.6%. Visa unfortunately did see a drop in market share over the last year. But it's still 60.79%; it is a giant in the space. And the overall number of transactions that it boasted of, 69.4 billion, comfortably exceeded the 63.8 billion reported the previous year.

Management certainly seems optimistic about 2026. They expect, for instance, for revenue to grow at a low-double-digit rate while earnings per share should match that. Unfortunately, we don't really know beyond this what the picture will look like. But any company growing at a double-digit rate is certainly promising. I know that there is a lot of uncertainty regarding the payments industry because of cryptocurrency and other changes to the financial technology space. Competition is rampant, and that does increase the risk profile for any player in the industry. However, management is eager to continue innovating.

On April 14th, as an example, the firm announced that it launched its own validator node on the Tempo network, marking continued advancement of blockchain infrastructure leadership and its role in the stablecoin space. In a previous article, I actually detailed how stablecoin could actually be of benefit to the company. And I have not seen anything that has come out since then that weakens my resolve in that regard. What makes Tempo particularly interesting is that Tempo was designed specifically for agentic commerce and machine-to-machine payments. In theory, Visa stands to benefit from this arrangement because, in exchange for serving as a lead validator that essentially packages transactions into blocks that will be added to the blockchain in order to authenticate payments, it will receive stablecoins. This alone will serve as an additional revenue and profit source for the business as time goes on and further cement its position as a leader in the stablecoin industry.

Earlier this month, management also announced a new solution called Intelligent Commerce Connect, which it describes as a service that will make it easier for businesses to connect to and participate in AI-powered commerce. It essentially operates as a network, a protocol, and a token vault agnostic on-ramp to agentic commerce for agent builders, merchants, and enablers. This is important because consumers are becoming increasingly reliant on AI agents in order to make purchases, and businesses are building agents, selling to them, and processing transactions as a result. Of course, the company is making investments in things that aren't necessarily cutting edge as well. On April 1st, for instance, the company announced six new dispute resolution tools aimed at reducing the billions of dollars lost each year to inefficient and outdated dispute processes. For context, in 2025 alone, the company processed 106 million disputes across the globe. That's 35% more than what it processed back in 2019. By deploying tools aimed at dealing with this challenge, the firm should ultimately boost its overall profitability in the long run. Though the extent to which this will be the case remains to be seen.

My only problem with the company is the fact that shares are anything but cheap. In the chart above, you can see how the company is priced using results from 2025 and how it stacks up against two other similar firms. Compared to these other two companies, it's certainly not expensive. But these are multiples that I consider to be high on an absolute basis. In fact, if this weren't a high-quality cash cow that's growing at a solid pace, I would even take a bearish stance at these levels. Given the nature of the business and its market position, a more neutral approach is justifiable.

Naturally, as more data becomes available, my overall view of the business will likely evolve. It just so happens that, on April 28th, management will be announcing financial results for the second quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. The current expectation analysts have is for revenue to come in at about $10.74 billion. Should this come to fruition, it would mean a year-over-year increase of 11.9% compared to the $9.59 billion that the business reported a year earlier. Earnings per share, meanwhile, have been forecasted to grow from $2.32 to $3.05. That would mean a jump in net profits from $4.58 billion to $5.90 billion. The table below shows certain cash flow metrics for the second quarter of 2025 as well. If revenue and profits do increase year over year, it is almost certain that most, if not all, of these will improve also.

Takeaway

Fundamentally speaking, I would say that Visa is a top-tier opportunity. However, it's not exactly the right prospect for me. Even though the business should do well in the long run, with continued innovations and a growth in the payment processing industry working in its favor, the stock is priced at levels that are difficult to justify. I won't go so far as to take a bearish stance on it. But I do think that maintaining it as a ‘hold’ is appropriate.

data.title: ServiceNow: Don't Throw The Baby Out With The Bathwater
data.description:
From Private Credit To Enterprise Software

Over the last few weeks, I have spent quite a bit of time researching the private credit industry, given all the headlines on the topic at the moment and its importance for the U.S. economy. My study started with the alternative asset managers Blue Owl Capital (OWL) and Ares Management (ARES). I also initiated coverage on multiple business development companies, including Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC), Ares Capital (ARCC), FS KKR (FSK), and the Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL). As I wrote in my recent article 'Blackstone Secured Lending: My Top Pick Among BDCs':

On April 20th, the credit rating agency Fitch also published a note mentioning that the Iran war and software disruption are the two biggest risks facing U.S. credit markets.

When we talk about private credit, we are necessarily talking about software, given that it is one of the main areas of concern. As an example, software companies account for 21% and 24% of BXSL's and ARCC's respective investment portfolios. Earlier this week, the leading software-focused private equity firm Thoma Bravo announced that it was pulling the plug on its $6.4 billion investment in Medallia, giving the keys back to its creditors, including Blackstone (BX), KKR (KKR) and Apollo (APO), among others. These ongoing developments highlight the close link between private equity and private credit, as if you are worried about the liabilities side of the balance sheet, the implications are quite clear for the equity slice.

In a way, woes in private credit and what is now termed the 'SaaS-pocalypse' are two sides of the same coin. When I think about market sentiment, software stocks are probably the most hated they have ever been, and that also applies to a market leader like ServiceNow (NOW). In my view, this is precisely where an opportunity lies. Starting in early February 2026, I have initiated multiple enterprise software companies with 'Strong Buy' ratings, including ServiceNow, Adobe (ADBE), Intuit (INTU) and Salesforce (CRM). Since then, the selling in the industry has continued pretty much unabated, and ServiceNow's stock price is down by ~10% since my initial coverage published on February 11th, 2026.

Following the release of the Q1-26 earnings results earlier this week, I thought it was an opportune time to write an update on the story. I believe the negative sentiment around software in general is creating a wonderful opportunity to acquire a leader like ServiceNow. As I wrote in February, not only do I consider NOW as largely immune from AI-related disruption risks, but I actually believe it could be a key beneficiary from this structural growth trend.

Based on my current understanding of the company's business model, I believe the market is currently throwing the baby out with the bathwater, as the ServiceNow AI Platform lays down the underlying architecture for artificial intelligence use cases within large enterprises and government entities.

Q1-26 Earnings Overview

ServiceNow reported its Q1-26 earnings last Wednesday after trading hours. The company's financial results exceeded previously issued guidance across a broad range of growth and profitability metrics. Despite still being early in the year, this prompted management to increase guidance for the full year of 2026.

With a beat and rise quarter, I was certainly not expecting to see the largest sell-off in the company's history, meaning since its initial public offering in June 2012. After reaching a high of $104.31 in intraday trading on Wednesday, NOW's share price collapsed to a low of $83.58 post-earnings, which is equivalent to a ~20% decline in just a few hours. The stock recovered some of its losses on Friday, but remains down by ~12.5% since Wednesday's closing price of $103.07 per share. In my view, one element that could have prompted such a large selloff is the narrative that ServiceNow is becoming increasingly dependent on acquisitions to maintain subscription revenue growth above 20%. As I will touch upon later, I don't think that such a punitive market reaction was warranted here.

Looking at the company's financial results, subscription revenues increased from $3,005 million in Q1-25 to $3,671 billion in the present quarter. This represents a 22% growth rate on a reported basis and a 19% increase on a constant currency basis. Remaining performance obligations also reached an impressive $27.7 billion, the equivalent of a 23.5% year-over-year growth rate on a constant currency basis. In turn, subscription revenues are now expected between $15,735 million and $15,775 million for the full-year 2026, a slight increase from the previous guidance range of $15,530 million to $15,570 million released at the end of January 2026.

ServiceNow also slightly exceeded expectations on margins, with an adjusted net income margin of 32.0% during the quarter compared to a prior guidance of 31.5%. Again, we are not talking about a material beat, but it is certainly in sharp contrast to the share price reaction post earnings.

The only element that was revised a tiny bit lower was the free cash flow margin, now expected at 35% for the full year of 2026 versus a previously guided 36% at the end of January 2026. No matter how I look at it, I have to admit that I am once again impressed by ServiceNow's financial results. I mean, we are talking about a $15.7 billion subscription revenue base growing in excess of 20% per year on a constant currency basis. Just think about the power of such a business model if operating expenses remain under control.

Recent Acquisitions

If we forget the AI-related disruption fears for a minute, another element that could raise questions in the minds of investors is the multiple acquisitions recently completed. To be more specific, ServiceNow completed the $506 million acquisition of Logik.io in May 2025. That was followed by the $2.4 billion acquisition of Moveworks in December 2025 and the $1.2 billion purchase of Veza in March 2026.

Just a few days ago, ServiceNow also closed a $7.8 billion deal for the cybersecurity firm Armis, marking the largest inorganic transaction in the company's history. From a bearish perspective, the argument would be that ServiceNow is buying revenues in an attempt to hide deteriorating fundamentals. If that were true, I think we would have seen a much greater deterioration in margins than what management just guided for fiscal 2026. As illustrated below, ServiceNow's margins are pretty much in line with previous years, and even higher when looking at free cash flows. On the other hand, the bullish case would be that ServiceNow is solidifying its core platform by incorporating additional functionalities in a rapidly evolving competitive landscape. As highlighted in the quote below, Armis and Veza now work together on an integrated platform. What I see here is strategy, the very opposite of merely buying revenues.

Together, Armis delivers real-time visibility and protection across every connected cyber asset, while Veza maps every permission and access path across human, machine, and AI agent identities.

Over the last year or so, ServiceNow also announced the acquisition of Data.World in May 2025, of Pyramid Analytics in February 2026 and of Traceloop in March 2026. Those transactions were much smaller in size, so the specific transaction terms were not disclosed. Going further back in time, we also find the tuck-in acquisitions of CueIn AI and Quality360 in January and February 2025, respectively.

According to ServiceNow's 2025 10-K filing, the company deployed $1.1 billion towards business combinations in fiscal 2025, a large increase versus the $279 million and $113 million disclosed in fiscal 2023 and 2024, respectively. With the $7.8 billion all-cash transaction with Armis, this number will necessarily reach a new high in fiscal 2026, hence why all of those acquisitions may raise some questions within the investment community. At the end of the day, even if some of those investments do not turn out as expected, I don't think it justifies the severe sell-off we have witnessed on a year-to-date basis. If one thing, I see all of those transactions are reinforcing the breadth and depth of the NOW platform. I also concede that doing so many deals is a relatively new strategy for management, as most of the growth in the past was organic in nature.

A Highly Attractive Valuation

Based on a consensus of 45 analysts' estimates, ServiceNow is expected to generate earnings per share of $4.13 in fiscal 2026. Taking into consideration the rapid growth of the underlying business, the consensus increases by about 21%, to reach $5.01 per share in fiscal 2027.

Using the closing price of $90.17 per share as of Friday's close, ServiceNow is currently trading at 21.8x and 18.0x the forward earnings for fiscal 2026 and 2027, respectively. As illustrated in the chart below, this is by far the lowest valuation multiple in the company's recent history and a world of difference versus the peak in excess of 60x recorded toward the end of 2024, when the stock was trading at all-time highs. Market expectations have been revised lower multiple times already, and I think it provides the perfect foundation to safely deploy capital at current market prices. At 60x earnings, the risk-reward proposition was asymmetric to the downside, but at 18x earnings, it has reversed to the upside. Even the best businesses in the world are not worth an infinite price.

As mentioned previously, management guided for subscription revenue of $15.7 billion in fiscal 2026 at the midpoint of the guidance range. Using the free cash flow margin of 35% also expected in fiscal 2026 would translate into a free cash flow figure of approximately $5.5 billion. Relative to ServiceNow's current market capitalization of $93.4 billion, we are then talking about a free cash flow yield of 5.9%. In my view, this is highly compelling given the speed at which the business is growing.

Using the Excel add-on Wisesheets, I have compiled ServiceNow's historical financial statements going all the way back to the early days of the company in 2009. When we take a step back from the daily fluctuations of the stock market and look at the historical financial results, what we see is truly impressive. Just add a few more years of double-digit growth, and ServiceNow will become a free cash flow machine. For those wanting to play around with the numbers in the table below, feel free to download the attached Excel file.

Conclusion

Despite the daily drop of -17.7% after earnings yesterday, ServiceNow delivered once again solid financial performance, exceeding the high-end of its previously announced guidance range in addition to increasing forecasts for fiscal 2026. Subscription revenues are once again expected to grow in excess of 20% this year, and nothing indicates that NOW's fabulous growth engine has stopped; quite the contrary.

AI-related fears of disruption are creating the opportunity to buy one of the highest-quality companies that I am aware of at a highly attractive price of 18.0x forward earnings for 2027. Over the long-term, I believe ServiceNow will be a net beneficiary from the ongoing AI deployment in the business world. In fact, it is precisely on a platform like the one offered by ServiceNow that the rubber meets the road, where artificial intelligence and large language models can actually make an impact on the day-to-day activities of a large enterprise.

My 'Strong Buy' rating reflects the fact that I have been slowly accumulating shares of ServiceNow over the last few days and weeks. I am particularly happy to have bought shares on April 10th at an average price of $82.44, although it remains to be seen if the low of $81.24 will hold. In a way, I would be quite happy if it does not, as buying ServiceNow in the high $70s would truly be a gift. In any case, the lower it goes, the more I am going to buy. As usual, prudence and patience will be required here, but I think ServiceNow will actually emerge stronger from the ongoing turmoil. Artificial intelligence is a massive opportunity for the NOW platform rather than a disruptive threat. This is exactly where my perspective diverges from what is currently implied by the market.

data.title: Palo Alto Networks: The AI Security Inflection Point Is Undervalued
data.description:
Setting The Stage

When you listen to most investors, there is a version of the AI story most of them are telling. That is the one about productivity, automation and large language models. However, there is another less told chapter. Every AI agent deployed in the enterprise expands the attack surface. With every autonomous workflow is a new lateral movement vector and every piece of AI generated code introduces potential vulnerabilities at machine speed.

Palo Alto Networks, Inc. (PANW) is not a mere beneficiary of this story. Arguably, it is the lateral beneficiary of it. As CEO Nikesh mentioned in the Q2 2026, AI agents becoming autonomous employees makes the old security playbook obsolete. To me that framing is not marketing. Rather it’s a structural tailwind underpinning a multiyear earnings ramp that the market is still pricing below my estimated fair value.

The stock is currently trading at about $179, this is below its early 2026 highs of about $194 following a post earnings pullback driven by a revised EPS guidance range.

I take it as a minor recalibration and not a thesis break as I will demonstrate. As such, I rate PANW a buy with a price target of about $216.

The Macro Perspective: Market & Industry Context

As of 2025, the global cybersecurity market stood at approximately $227.6 billion and is projected to hit $351.9 billion by 2030 marking a CAGR of 9.1%.

Looking at it, I think the headline understates the opportunity for platform native leaders like PANW which are growing revenues at more than 15% TTM nearly double the market rate.

I find the structural drivers to be durable. Take for example Ransomware supply chain attacks, zero-day exploits and AI-augmented phishing are no longer edge cases. Instead they are constant operating conditions for nearly every enterprise. Elsewhere, the SEC’s cybersecurity disclosure rules are forcing public companies to treat security not as an AI line item but as a board level governance obligation. This is simply telling us what was largely a discretionary security spending is turning into non-discretionary spending hence a win for the cybersecurity market.

With this massive market opportunity, PANW is perfectly positioned to seize it. Palo Alto recently introduced Prisma AIRS which is its AI runtime security product. In Q2 2026, Prisma AIRS tripled its customer base to over 100 showing massive market adoption and acceptance. Separately, PANW was named among founding members of Anthropic’s project Glasswing cybersecurity initiative thus cementing PANW’s position at the intersection of AI development and AI defense.

I also see a strong tailwind in the geopolitical space with state sponsored cyberattacks escalating across sectors and critical infrastructure. I view this to be a durable floor under the government security budget that is unlikely to compress even in a downturn.

A Deeper Dive: The Fundamental Assessment

I will start from the topline. PANW’s 2025 revenue came in at $9.2 billion a 14.9% YoY growth mapping a consistent double digit revenue growth streak spanning not less than 5 years now. Q2 2026 saw a revenue of $2.6 billion, a 14.9% YoY growth with a full year guidance raised to $11.28-$11.31 billion, a 22-23% growth acceleration in the back half of the fiscal year.

Crucially, the growth is not just a topline velocity but a quality growth. I say this in the sense that the remaining performance obligation[RPO] which is a leading indicator of future revenue hit a record $16 billion in Q2, up by about 23% YoY. This speaks to a clear solid revenue visibility and a testament to the company's strong market appeal.

That notwithstanding, I think the most apparent proof of PANW’s platform consolidation thesis is its next generation security [NGS] ARR which reached $6.3 billion in Q2 marking a 33% YoY growth. The management guides NGS ARR of $7.94-7.96 for 2026 FY which implies a 56% YoY growth. Being subscription based, I see this not as a cyclical demand but a contractually embedded revenue compounding at scale. Given the strong market tailwinds discussed previously, I think the trend is likely to continue beyond 2026.

Lastly is the margin aspect. The company delivered a non-GAAP operating margin of 30.3% in Q2 for the third consecutive quarter of its operating margin being above 30%. To me, this margin consistency signals operational maturity not a onetime optimization. The management’s target of a 40% free cash flow margin by 2028 implies a substantial operating leverage still ahead as revenue base keeps scaling up.

PANW’s trailing operating cash flow stands at close to $4 billion with a gross profit margin hovering in the north of 70% and an operating margin above 12%. This speaks to the company’s strong operational leverage.

Looking at the consensus revenue for 2026 and 2027 in the region of 20%, I see an alignment with my bullish investment thesis anchored on solid market drivers and growing customer base.

The consensus EPS with consistent positive growth figures signal a continued momentum of profitability.

In a nutshell, PANW's current fundamentals are strong, intact and the outlook remains solidly in line with my bullish thesis.

My Overview Of Management's Guidance

Post the Q2 2026 the management raised full year revenue guidance from about $10.5 billion to $11.28 billion-$11.31 billion. This is about $800 million upward revision which is a reflection of genuine platformization demand. Q3 guidance calls for 28%-29% revenue growth and NGS ARR of $7.94-$7.96 billion, a 56% YoY growth. To me this is a signal that the recurring revenue base is accelerating, not plateauing.

Further, the management's EPS trimmed guidance from $3.80-$3.90 to about $3.65-$3.70 didn't sit well with investors and this in my view, explains the stock's pullback post earnings. I view it differently though. I see it as a reflection of deliberate investment in CyberArk and Chronosphere integration and not a deterioration in underlying demand.

A notable metric is the longterm NGS ARR target of $20 billion by 2030 and reaffirmation of the 40% FCF margin by 2028. When a company trims near term EPS while holding a solid longterm ARR target, the right read is investment and not impairment.

The Cybersecurity Correction: Panic Or Opportunity?

The cybersecurity stocks have experienced two AI driven selloffs in 2026. The first one came in February when Anthropic launched Claude Code security and the second hit recently after leaks of the Claude Mythos model rattled the sector.

I view this as a mispricing at least for PANW. The fear conflates code scanning tools with wholesale replacements for runtime detection, cloud security and network firewalls. This is the core of what PANW offers. More AI deployment means more attack surface to secure not less. The same wave that scared investors is accelerating enterprise demand for exactly what this company delivers. At its current valuation and the $215.66 price estimate I will discuss shortly, the correction has simply created a better entry point.

Valuation

Owing to the company’s consistent double digit growth revenue growth, accelerating NGS ARR and solid margins, I think Ben Graham’s intrinsic value formula suits this company's valuation. I believe the methodology is appropriate here due to its strong emphasis on earnings and growth. Given this background, the formula is as defined below.

Using the above formula, I estimate a fair value of $215.66 which is a 20.79% undervaluation assuming the 2026 consensus EPS of $3.69 and a growth rate of 22%. The growth rate is within management’s guided revenue growth and the consensus revenue growth for 2026 and 2027 mentioned above.

I think this valuation is justified by NGS ARR compounding, $16 billion RPO visibility and a FCF margin trajectory approaching 40%. For these reasons, I am rating this stock a buy.

Risks

While I am bullish here, it comes with a risk too. First off, PANW business relies on enterprise consolidating their security vendors onto its platforms. If economic pressure causes CISOs to defer consolidation, or Microsoft accelerates its bundled security push, PANW’s cross-sell velocity could slow down.

Secondly, although security is increasingly non-discretionary, I think large platform consolidation decisions can be deferred for a while in a downturn. A material slowdown in enterprise IT budgets poses a potential short to mid-term risk.

Conclusion

I am rating PANW a buy at ~21% discount. The tailwinds are durable and the fundamentals are sound. The valuation gap is worth seizing.

data.title: ELD: Current Income And Lower Dollar Beneficiary
data.description:
Introduction

After strong gains in 2025, the WisdomTree Emerging Markets Local Debt Fund ETF (ELD) has slipped in early 2026, impacted by broad-based weakness in fixed-income instruments amid energy price volatility. At the same time, the United States' status as an energy exporter has helped the USD appreciate relative to the currencies of energy-importing countries in emerging markets. As I will discuss in this article, these countries account for the majority of ELD holdings.

These dynamics potentially create a buying opportunity in ELD for investors with an H2 2027/H1 2028 horizon. The bullish investment thesis behind ELD can be summarized as:

  • ELD holdings are principally investment grade, making the 7.19% yield to maturity quite attractive as long as currency risks do not materialize.
  • The Fed should be in a comfortable position to resume rate cuts in H2 2027, putting pressure on the USD exchange rate relative to emerging market currencies.
  • In the meantime, my 12-month return outlook of 6.00-6.50% in USD terms should be quite attractive for investors comfortable with modest interest rate risk.

ETF Strategy and Characteristics

ELD is an actively managed ETF investing in local currency bonds issued by emerging market governments and government agencies. Looking at the WisdomTree website, we see that the ETF currently has modest interest rate risk (effective duration of 4.65x, meaning a 1% change in rates will have a circa 4.65% impact on ELD's portfolio value). Likewise, credit risk remains arguably limited, with over 75% of issuers rated investment grade, while another ~20% are rated BB, the highest rating in the high-yield category.

As such, currency risk remains a principal risk in the investment case, with U.S. dollar returns largely driven by the change of the USD exchange rate relative to major ELD holdings such as the Indonesian rupiah, Brazilian real, and Malaysian ringgit.

The potential for currency losses is somewhat offset by higher coupons for local currency emerging market debt, with ELD's portfolio offering a 7.19% yield to maturity, achieved with a weighted average maturity of 7.22 years. For comparison, U.S. 7-year bonds currently offer a yield of about 4.1%, reflecting notably higher return potential in local emerging markets debt as long as currency risks do not materialize.

Last but not least, as with most active ETFs, ELD's expense ratio is rather high at 0.55%. This is somewhat justified by the hard access individual investors typically have to local currency debt of emerging markets. Even so, the high expense ratio arguably makes ELD more of a tactical holding during periods of expected U.S. dollar weakness, as I will discuss in the next section.

U.S. Dollar Outlook

With the Department of Justice dropping the probe into Chair Powell, I think a speedy confirmation of Kevin Warsh in the next few weeks will follow, ultimately allowing for a new Fed chair to take the reins in June 2026. Alas, the war in Iran has dashed initial hopes for a swift rate cut, with Fed funds futures pricing reflecting a return to easing in H2 2027.

Also taking into account the status of the United States as a net energy exporter, a key benefit as long as Middle East tensions continue, I think the U.S. dollar should move sideways over the next 12 months before losing ground as the Fed gains confidence to continue with its policy normalization in 2027. This in turn should benefit ELD's net asset value, akin to what happened in 2025.

In the meantime, I think investors will be able to collect ELD's attractive current dividends, as I will highlight in the next section.

ELD Return Outlook

ELD's dividend yield currently stands at 5.63%, making it a key pillar of ELD's total returns. Taking note of the 6.03% average coupon for ELD's portfolio, I expect dividend growth to continue in the near term, although the negative 1.12% growth rate over the past decade highlights that dividend growth is not a major contributor to ELD returns, especially when we factor in currency moves.

The 7.19% yield to maturity also reflects some capital gain potential, only partially offset by the ETF's 0.55% expense ratio. As such, I see a circa 6.00-6.50% total USD return as achievable over the next 12 months (reflective of a sideways movement in the U.S. dollar), with somewhat higher potential returns potentially attainable over the subsequent 12 months (i.e., H2 2027-H1 2028), when the resumption of Fed rate cuts should bring renewed pressure on the U.S. dollar exchange rate.

All in all, I think this return setup is overall attractive for income-focused investors, ranking ELD with a Buy rating. An added near-term catalyst for ELD may prove to be political uncertainty ahead of the U.S. midterm elections in November, although I believe that markets will remain largely focused on the Fed policy outlook rather than developments in Washington.

Risks

As mentioned in the ETF Strategy and Characteristics section, I believe that currency risk is the principal risk in the investment case. Indeed, while outright defaults by countries where ELD invests appear somewhat unlikely, USD returns may fall well below expectations if we see emerging market currencies weaken relative to the Greenback. This may principally occur during periods of financial and economic turmoil, when capital tends to flow to safe haven assets such as the USD and away from riskier emerging market currencies. As such, investors in ELD should be comfortable with mark-to-market losses on their holdings during such circumstances.

In the near term, I should note ELD is principally invested in energy-importing countries in Asia and Eastern Europe, with currencies in these economies likely to come under pressure if we see renewed fighting in Iran. While I remain hopeful that a diplomatic solution will ultimately be found, long-lasting conflicts such as the war in Ukraine show that geopolitical tensions could take many years to be resolved.

Last but not least, as with any fixed-income investment with a duration longer than a few months, investors need to take note of the possibility of losses if emerging market central banks hike rates to combat inflation. Indeed, this may result in significant near-term losses for ELD investors, although they should ultimately be recouped as the ETF's holdings mature and prices return to par value. Hence, such periods can arguably be characterized by mark-to-market capital losses to be offset by future dividend growth, akin to what happened in the years following 2022.

Conclusion

After solid gains in 2025, ELD has slipped in early 2026, impacted by a mix of broad-based weakness in fixed-income instruments as well as a marginally stronger U.S. dollar relative to the currencies of energy importers in emerging markets.

I expect ELD to deliver a total return of about 6.00-6.50% over the next 12 months, subsequently followed by a one-off gain as the Fed resumes its policy normalization in H2 2027 and into H1 2028. I believe these returns should be overall attractive for fixed-income investors, ranking ELD with a Buy rating.

Currency and interest rate risks should be on the radar for ELD investors, with any further escalation in the war in Iran potentially resulting in ELD underperforming my near-term return outlook.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: ACV: Discounted Valuation Means It's Time To Buy (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Overview

2026 was off to a rough start as both the equity and debt markets experienced headwinds. Since Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund (ACV) has exposure to both of these markets, it negatively impacted performance. At the time of my last coverage, I issued a hold rating because the fund was too expensive at the time. However, I believe that the market pullback has provided us with an opportunity to begin accumulating again. Furthermore, ACV has released an updated annual report that I wanted to use to reassess the fund's performance, earnings growth, and outlook for the remainder of the year.

When I previously covered ACV, the fund traded at a premium to NAV of 5.69%. Following the decline through the first quarter of the year, ACV now trades at a more attractive discount to NAV of 3.49%. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that ACV now sits at the more attractive end of its historical price-to-NAV range. For reference, ACV has traded at an average discount to NAV of 2.21% over the last five years. Therefore, this can present an opportunity for long-term investors to begin accumulating shares in anticipation of a turnaround in the debt markets.

ACV now offers investors a high starting dividend yield of 8.2% while issuing those payouts on a monthly basis. After assessing the latest reporting, it is clear that the fund's strategy can generate sufficient earnings to support these payouts. However, there are some clear tradeoffs that investors must consider. For instance, the distributions are mostly being funded by short-term capital gains, which means that ACV may be vulnerable to a downturn in the market and a higher tax burden. Despite this, I still anticipate the long-term performance of the fund to remain solid.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest portfolio overview, ACV has total managed assets of $387.6M that are allocated across a blend of fixed-income securities and equities. The primary objective is to provide an attractive total return that is made up of the combination of a high current income and capital appreciation. To achieve this goal, the fund specifically allocates the majority of its assets to convertible securities, which are hybrid instruments that generate income in a bond-like fashion but have the ability to be converted to common shares if management believes the position has growth potential. The fund's breakdown by asset allocation is as follows:

  • Convertible Securities: 58.90%
  • Common Equities: 21.62%
  • High-Yield Bonds: 14.07%
  • Cash & Equivalents: 5.27%

While the fund isn't specifically trying to mimic any index, it does provide an attractive level of diversity across different sectors. ACV is currently most heavily allocated to information technology positions at 30.05% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to industrial and financial positions, which accounts for 11.13% and 10.42% of assets, respectively. The convertible structure is ideal during this time when markets are punishing software companies. So while traditional software stocks are declining, ACV has the ability to continue generating income from the sector regardless.

When assessing the top holdings within ACV, we can see that the fund provides exposure to some of the highest-quality companies in the world. For instance, we can see that ACV has traditional equity exposure to companies like NVIDIA (NVDA), Apple (AAPL), and Alphabet (GOOG). Additionally, ACV has exposure to strong companies through its convertible exposure, including companies like Lumentum Holdings (LITE), Western Digital Corporation (WDC), Welltower (WELL), and Wells Fargo (WFC), just to name a few. Since ACV has this exposure through convertibles, the fund may not be able to directly participate in the growth of these holdings.

Performance Expectations

Since my last coverage, ACV released an updated annual report for 2026 that provides some transparency into its earnings. Be aware that the reporting cycle for ACV is a bit weird, as their fiscal year ends in January. Net investment income amounted to $0.05 per share, while net realized gains landed at $5.09 per share. Looking back over the last few years, the fund's level of net investment income has always been a bit disappointing, which is a surprise since the majority of the portfolio is structured as income-producing convertible securities.

While this structure isn't necessarily a red flag, it does make me question the overall efficiency of the convertible strategy. Historically, the fund has been more efficient at capturing large net realized gains through market rallies. These net realized gains are the main driver behind the NAV growth and the fund's ability to support distributions. While this is great during bull markets, this is also a vulnerability during market declines.

For instance, the fund suffered a large net realized loss of $3.65 per share for its 2023 period. This large net realized loss significantly reduced the NAV, and the fund still hasn't actually recovered from it, despite stronger earnings in the last two years. For reference, here are the annual NAV amounts over the last few years:

  • 2022: $27.25 per share
  • 2023: $21.22 per share
  • 2024: $20.85 per share
  • 2025: $23.31 per share
  • 2026: $25.91 per share

This sort of dynamic means that ACV has to actively sell off the upside growth of its strongest positions to support payouts. So over a longer holding period, this has translated to limited capital appreciation over time. For instance, ACV's share price has increased by about 45.7% over the last decade, despite market indices rallying to all-time highs. When including all distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 342.7% over the same time frame. Therefore, investors can anticipate that most of their returns will come from ACV's ability to maintain a high rate of distributions over time.

The ideal scenario for ACV would be slower growth of the indices and a lower interest rate environment. This should improve the fund's operating spread between its cost of debt and the yield collected from its positions since leverage is used. ACV has a total effective leverage of 27.14% of assets, which is equivalent to ~$105M. While this is a moderate use of leverage, it can still increase the risk profile of the fund. For instance, leverage may be allocated to securities that decline in value, which would then amplify losses.

However, ACV has already proven that it can participate in the upside growth of the market while also outperforming during periods of flat movement. As the S&P 500 (SPY) sold off technology companies over the last two quarters, ACV was able to benefit from the rotation of capital and outperform. For instance, ACV's total return sits above 47% over the last twelve months, which outpaces SPY's total return of about 32.1% for the same time frame.

So looking forward, I expect ACV to be able to participate in the ongoing market recovery as concerns around geopolitical tensions and AI start to fade. ACV still has a meaningful allocation to technology holdings, and management has a good track record of picking securities that can lead to gains within its portfolio. While ACV may not be the most efficient way to capture this growth over the long term, I think it is attractive for retired investors that want a bit of both: growth and income generation.

Risk

The biggest risk seems to be the fund's reliance on positive market momentum. Since net investment income isn't an efficient part of the portfolio strategy, the fund has no way of protecting earnings during market declines. An ideal structure for ACV would be for net investment income to substantially improve and reduce the fund's reliance on capturing net realized gains. This shift in strategy would allow better capital appreciation since ACV would not need to sell off the growth of its positions.

Additionally, this would reduce the severity of declines during unfavorable market conditions. For instance, ACV's share price experienced a drastic downturn in 2022, which was more severe than the S&P 500. Even though ACV offers a high dividend yield, this doesn't automatically result in better performance during downturns. Through 2022's downturn, ACV had a total loss of 36%, which was nearly double the losses that SPY experienced over the same time frame.

The current structure puts ACV at the risk of paying out more than it earns. If we ever face a scenario of a prolonged downturn in the market, it can be disastrous for ACV's NAV health. Furthermore, I want to emphasize that ACV makes the most sense for investors that specifically want to focus on income generation. If you're an investor who wants to maximize total return potential, ACV may not be a great fit for you.

Improved Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.18 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 8.2%. Assuming that ACV keeps distributions unchanged for the remainder of the year, we can estimate an annual payout amount of $2.16 per share. Referring back to the same annual report, it is clear that ACV has no issues generating sufficient earnings to support these payouts. For instance, ACV reported total earnings of $5.14 per share for the 2026 period, which is actually enough to support multiple years' worth of payouts.

Looking at the payout history below, we can see that ACV also issued a large supplemental distribution of $0.384 per share at the end of 2025. This was likely a result of management rewarding shareholders for the excellent performance the fund had for the year. Since performance is likely to be strong for the next period, assuming market indices continue to trend higher, there is certainly the possibility that ACV will issue another supplemental payout at the end of this year.

These supplementals are great for investors seeking income generation within their portfolio. However, I also think it would be a great move to retain this capital and invest in new positions. This alternative route can possibly lead to greater NAV growth for investors, which would increase the overall total returns provided. This is just a nitpick and isn't crucial to the fund's success in the future.

However, I do want to mention that the distributions received from ACV may not offer any tax efficiency. For instance, the latest available Section 19(a) notice indicates that 94.6% of the distributions paid YTD were supported by net realized short-term capital gains, which are classified as ordinary dividends and have the highest tax burden for investors. This also supports the idea that ACV is actively selling off the growth of its strongest positions. Therefore, a position in ACV may be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors that want to minimize their overall tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am upgrading my rating on ACV to a buy at this time. The fund now trades at an attractive discount to NAV valuation, and the latest annual report indicates that the distributions are supported by earnings. In a scenario where the market indices continue to rally through the remainder of the year, I believe that ACV is positioned to capitalize on this positive momentum. However, investors need to be aware that the high yield is ultimately reliant on ACV's ability to capture net realized gains. Net investment income levels are a bit disappointing, so there is definitely room for improvement in this regard.

data.title: YMAG: Don't Trade Away The Mag 7 Upside Now
data.description:
One of the most important weeks for the broad market is on tap. We’ll hear from four of the Magnificent Seven stocks, with an emphasis on the major AI hyperscalers. Key inflation data and the April FOMC meeting are tossed into the mix, while the conflict in Iran presses on. You’d think that implied volatility would be elevated going into such a consequential final week of April. Not so.

In fact, the Cboe Volatility Index (VIX) is under 20, and well below its March high of 35. That also means there’s not much juice to squeeze out of selling call options on the largest US stocks. That brings us to the YieldMax Magnificent 7 Fund of Option Income ETF (YMAG). Holding option-income funds on each of the Mag 7 names, I don’t think now is an opportune time to get long.

I reiterate a hold rating. The product has returned almost 30% since my July 2024 analysis, but that merely matches the S&P 500 Index’s advance (including dividends on the SPX, and YMAG lags).

Today, I’ll run through the state of the Mag 7, along with a nod to volatility readings, underscoring my neutral stance.

Earnings Ahead

YMAG Lags MAGS Over the Past 2 Years (Total Returns)

According to the issuer, YMAG invests its assets in seven YieldMax ETFs. The fund’s name refers to its strategy of gaining exposure to the common stocks of seven companies, collectively known as the “Magnificent Seven”. The portfolio is reallocated monthly so that each of the seven YieldMax ETFs is equally weighted. The fund has a limited operating history, and while the portfolio’s objective is to provide current income, there is no guarantee the ETF will make a distribution. Distributions are likely to vary greatly in amount.

YMAG is a small ETF, with total assets under management of just $320 million, though that is more than triple the AUM since my previous assessment. The annual expense ratio is very high at 1.34%, which is a major drag to consider. While the stated dividend rate is 51%, prospective investors must keep in mind that the income is generated from trading away potential upside. Hence, the yield is absolutely not a free lunch.

Share-price momentum is rated merely lukewarm by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. The ETF Risk Grade is likewise not ideal, given somewhat high historical volatility and a downtrending price. Investors should always gauge total returns, not just the price return, however. Finally, on liquidity, YMAG scores well. Average daily volume is north of 1.1 million shares, while the median 30-day bid/ask spread is decent at 8 basis points. Prospective investors may still want to use limit orders around the market open.

Turning to the Mag 7. They are much cheaper today compared to last October and back in 2020. I like WisdomTree’s Daily Dashboard, which displays each of the stock’s key valuation metrics. For instance, even with NVIDIA (NVDA) notching a fresh all-time high last Friday, its forward price-to-earnings ratio is barely above that of the S&P 500.

Mag 7 Valuation Fundamentals

Aggregated, Ed Yardeni calls out that the Mag 7 is under 27x earnings, which is down from 31x late last year (even after the remarkable Nasdaq 100 recovery in April). The gap between the Mag 7’s earnings multiple and that of the “Other 493” is much narrower than historical trends.

So, I would actually rather own something like the Roundhill Magnificent Seven ETF (MAGS) right now. That would be my “buy” alternative to YMAG today.

Mag 7's P/E Is Well Below the 2025 Peak

What I found remarkable was the current S&P 500 drawdown from 52-week high heat map. We see that NVDA, Amazon (AMZN), and Alphabet (GOOGL) were once all down near or more than 20%. Last week, they settled at/near all-time highs. Of course, Microsoft (MSFT), Tesla (TSLA), and to a lesser extent Apple (AAPL) are still in drawdowns.

The upshot here is that the Mag 7 are leading the market’s recovery, so why trade away the upside? MAGS over YMAG is the better idea right now, in my view.

S&P 500 Drawdown From 52-Week High Heat Map

And here’s the implied volatility view. Notice in the chart below that MAGS’ implied volatility is now under its historical volatility. That suggests selling premium is simply not as lucrative as it was throughout Q1 (when IV was above HV).

Moreover, the IV’s absolute level is under 30%, which is a bit low compared to the historical average.

MAGS: Implied Volatility Declines, Limiting Yield Potential

The Technical Take

Quickly on MAGS’ technical chart. It’s not an all-clear yet. While the S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100 are at records, MAGS is below its October 2025 peak of 69. The current pattern is a bull flag, though, which is constructive, while its long-term 200-day moving average is on the rise (also bullish).

The downside gaps boxed in orange in the graph below could get filled, but it’s key to remember that not all gaps get filled in strong bull markets.

The Bottom Line

I have a hold rating on YMAG. I would rather own MAGS here, given the absolute and relative recent strength in the Mag 7. The group of glamor stocks’ collective valuation is low, earnings growth is high, and its leader (NVDA) is making new highs—that may be a harbinger of MAGS upside (which would result in YMAG negative alpha).

data.title: CEF Market Weekly Review: Muni And Loan CEF Incomes Diverge
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our CEF Market Weekly Review, where we discuss closed-end fund [CEF] market activity from both the bottom-up - highlighting individual fund news and events - as well as the top-down - providing an overview of the broader market. We also try to provide some historical context as well as the relevant themes that look to be driving markets or that investors ought to be mindful of.

This update covers the period through the third week of April. Be sure to check out our other weekly updates covering the business development company [BDC] as well as the preferreds/baby bond markets for perspectives across the broader income space.

Market Action

It was a strong week for BDCs as nearly all sectors continued rallying on expectations that the Iran conflict is coming to a close. MLPs was the only sector moving decisively lower on the week as oil prices fell.

CEF discounts continued to tighten past their historic average level.

Market Themes

CEF coverage figures were updated through February in the usual place (CEF Tool, Coverage tab). The main theme here is the drop in short-term rates and the pass-through to both the asset and liability sides of CEF balance sheets. Municipal CEF NII has increased over the past six months and that of Loan CEFs has fallen, as we would expect given the trajectory of the Fed Funds rate.

The market’s base case is for the Fed to remain on hold for the rest of the year.

If that’s correct, then NII across the CEF space will stabilize, at least through 2026. Some loan CEFs have been reducing distributions to align with the drop in NII. Those funds that haven’t like VVR, FCT, FRA, BGT are more vulnerable in this respect.

Muni CEFs hiked their distributions a couple of years ago, more in response to CEF activism rather than anything else, which means there is not a lot of room for them to hike distributions in response to higher NII. Most funds feature distribution coverage well below 100%.

Market Commentary

Flaherty preferred CEFs hiked their distributions slightly. As discussed in the recent article this is a trend that will likely continue. If the Fed goes on pause for the remainder of the year then we shouldn’t expect further hikes. However, if the market is correct that the Fed will deliver around 2 more rate cuts this cycle, then the Flaherty CEF suite should also deliver more hikes next year. Distribution hikes should be followed with valuation re-rating as well which is what we have seen in the past.

The Credit Suisse HY Credit Fund (DHY) announced a 3:1 transferable rights offering (DHY RT). The subscription price will be determined on 14-May and will be at a 7.5% discount to market price, floored at a 14% discount to NAV.

The fund's price fell in the wake of the news - a common theme during rights offerings and reflects both the expected NAV dilution as well as general investor aversion to rights offerings.

The NAV dilution should come in around 3.5%. This is how much the price should underperform the NAV, all else equal. This means that the pre-RO 8% discount should widen towards 12% before the NAV adjusts at expiry. The fund will start looking attractive closer to a 15% discount, in our view.

Both DHY and its sister CEF CIK have stumbled over the past year, reversing their historic outperformance in the sector. They delivered a total NAV return of around 6% in the last year vs. around 12% for the average HY corporate bond CEF.

The funds are still outperformers in the context of the last 5 years, however. CIK has an unusually low management fee and so consistently trades at a tighter discount to DHY and the sector average. If that changes it will be an attractive hold.

Stance and Takeways

This week we rotated and upsized our holding in the Flaherty preferred CEF FFC. The fund was trading at an unusually tight valuation versus our previous Flaherty CEF holding PFO. We expect the fund to continue to hike distributions in response to lower Fed Fund rates which should also result in a tightening of its discount over time.

data.title: Safran: The Disconnect Between Fundamentals And Price
data.description:
Safran SA (SAFRF, SAFRY) has lost nearly 20% since my last report, sharply underperforming the S&P 500’s 4.7% gain. The situation in the Middle East does provide a point of concern particularly for aftermarket sales, but as I discuss in this report the impact should have minimal impact on Safran this year. In this report, I discuss the company’s latest results, the guidance and risks and update my price target.

Safran Sees Double-Digit Sales Growth

Safran is one of the companies that only reports detailed earnings once and every six months. For Q1 and Q3, the company only discloses revenue. Revenues increased 18.8% to €8.6 billion. That included 23% organic growth to €8.9 billion on constant currency with a net negative impact from currency impact and work scope changes. The company saw strong growth in OEM sales at 22.1% as well as services with 23.8% growth.

Propulsion sales grew 33.1% organically and 23.6% to €4.6 billion driven by 43% growth in LEAP engine revenues per flight hour and 29% growth in spare part sales. Furthermore, we saw the military engine M88 increase volumes 6.5x. It is not a high-volume business, but it does show increased defense demand for engines. Equipment & Defense sales grew 13.5% organically and 21% reported sales growth to €3.4 billion driven by higher sales for electrical systems and wiring on the Boeing 737 MAX (BA), nacelles on the A320neo (EADSF), landing gear on the Boeing 787 and A330neo and higher defense sales in optronics and PNT (Position, Navigation and Time). Aircraft interior sales grew 9.2% organically but declined 11% to €700 million in reported sales driven by the divestment of Safran’s Passenger Innovation business.

Safran Confirms Guidance For 2026

Safran has confirmed its guidance presented earlier this year. That means that the company is still guiding for low to mid-teens growth in revenues driven by aftermarket sales and LEAP engine deliveries increasing with recurring operating income in the €6.1-€6.2 billion range and free cash flow of €4.4-€4.6 billion. The company did add the potential impact of the Middle East conflict as a new watch item adding it to a watchlist that already included supply chain risks.

Important to keep in mind is that even after a strong first quarter driven by a heavier work scope on shop visits, Safran did not alter its guidance. One reason is the uncertainty that conflict in the Middle East brings. The other reason is that Safran tends to not alter its guidance during the discussions for Q1 and Q3. So far, the company has seen no indication of slowdown, and they do not expect anything in Q2 either. Raw material costs have increased but Safran also has an inventory of materials and room to absorb higher materials costs. Given that there is some lag between significant financial impacts for airlines and Safran as a tier 1 aerospace and defense supplier, potential pressures could mostly be 12-18 months from now. In Q1, aftermarket services also increased partially driven by higher cabin MRO activities for the Middle Eastern airlines.

A tailwind that Safran enjoys is on tariffs. The company initially estimated a €100 million impact, but following the Supreme Court ruling on tariffs, the impact is now expected to be less than €50 million and Safran will reclaim up to €100 million.

Safran Stock Has Upside Even When Applying A Depressed Valuation Multiple

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Safran, I have accounted for any risks through a reset on the EV/EBITDA multiple to 15.8x, which is in line with the 10-year EV/EBITDA. This is below the company’s 18.8x 3-year median and substantially below the peer group multiple of more than 24x. Compounded growth rates through 2028 are still expected to be strong with 10.8% sales growth. 10.1% EBITDA growth and 7.7% free cash flow growth. Free cash flow growth mainly falls behind due to investments required to keep scaling the business for commercial aviation and defense growth. Even at a discounted multiple, we still see 27% upside with a $404.58 price target. So, when applying a rather aggressive discount of 15% on the EV/EBITDA multiple we still see upside for Safran.

In 2025, we saw Boeing substantially increasing its production rates and that showed in the margins rather quickly. For 2026, additional investments are likely to trigger a dip in the margins, but the free cash flow generation remains robust with cash conversion ratios expected to be around 60% in the years ahead and capital expenditures increasing to around 5% of revenues and around 25%-30% of operating cash flow .We note that the company is in a net cash position. That is a phenomenal position to be in and that is in no way reflected in the company’s current share price.

Conclusion: Safran Remains Underappreciated By The Market

There definitely is risk for Safran due to the situation in the Middle East and that risk more than likely be concentrated in 2028. However, we note that Safran remains undervalued against its peers and even at a discounted trading multiple still provides substantial upside. At current prices, I believe that Safran’s critical tier 1 supplier status with a strong balance sheet is not accurately reflected. As a result, I am maintaining my strong buy rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Organon: What The Buyout Rumors Mean For The Stock
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Organon's shares (OGN) skyrocketed by 30% after news of a possible takeover of the company. Although the market reacted positively, it still evaluates the probability of a deal as just a realistic rather than a baseline scenario. This is confirmed by the fact that even after a strong jump in price, the stock is still trading at a discount to the implied buyout valuation. I recommended the Hold rating in the last article, as I did not see any serious drivers for business reassessment. Now the driver has appeared, but it's more of an M&A catalyst rather than fundamental business improvements. The probability of a deal failure is high, but there are no specifics about its parameters at the moment and none of the parties have confirmed their interest. If the deal is implemented, the upside does exist, but it is more likely a speculative growth on the M&A deal. I keep the Hold rating because the business situation has not fundamentally changed and there is still a lot of uncertainty about a potential deal.

What Is Actually Known About the Buyout Talks

I recommend reading my first three articles about Organon, among other things. There, in addition to financial analysis of the reports, I also deeply analyzed the company's business model, described its development strategy, the strengths and weaknesses of this strategy, and the key growth drivers and risks inherent in the company. This will help you gain a deeper understanding of the business and better understand the reasons for the company's current financial situation. And now I'll move on to a potential deal.

On April 24, the media published news about the possible takeover of Organon by the Indian pharmaceutical giant Sun Pharmaceutical Industries. After the news was published, Organon's shares skyrocketed by 30%. However, the deal has not been confirmed by either party at the moment. The Economic Times writes that SunPharma has submitted a reported binding offer to buy Organon for $13 billion. Moreover, according to media reports, the Indian giant is not even the only interested party. The German Grunenthal and the Swedish private equity fund EQT are mentioned as potentially interested in the takeover of Organon companies. This is, in fact, all the information currently available. There are still many unanswered questions – the specific terms of the deal are not known, the terms of the deal are unknown, we don't even know if SunPharma has actually made an offer and whether the Organon board of directors is ready to approve the deal.

Despite this, I would like to reflect on the probability of this transaction, the valuation, and what investors should do in such a situation. The first thing I want to note is that I fully understand the interest of other companies in Organon. Despite its rather high debt burden, Organon was trading at huge discounts on multiples both to the sector and to its historical averages. I wrote about this back in February, and a week before the news was released, the shares fell even more - by almost 12%. Roughly speaking, the buyer gets for cheap a large company with a ready-made global sales infrastructure, a strong presence in the international market (especially in developing countries) and a good portfolio of brands that generate stable cash flow. For Indian SunPharma, which is actively doing business in emerging markets, it is very interesting to buy Organon at such a price, 80% of whose revenue is generated outside the United States. The synergy in the form of revenue growth and cost optimization seems quite realistic. Therefore, I consider the interest in the company to be well-founded, and the likelihood that the process is actually being considered seems significant to me. Especially considering that this is not the first wave of rumors, and there has also been news about the purchase of the company for $12 billion before. Organon stood out from Merck, but has not yet coped with the high debt burden and stagnating revenues, so selling the business seems like a logical way out of a difficult situation.

Breaking Down the $13B Valuation

In this chapter, I want to comment on the business assessment announced in the news. It was said that Organon wants to be acquired for $ 13 billion. Most likely, we are talking about Enterprise Value, since the company's capitalization is 6 times lower. After a sharp jump of 30%, the capital structure looks like this:

When estimating the entire company, including debt and excluding cache (I took the data at the end of Q4) at $12-13 billion, the equity value is about $3.77-4.77 billion, and the share price is in the range of $14.5-18.3 with an upside to the current price of 28-62%. This is a rough estimate depending on final terms and net debt. The fact that EV has still not reached $13 billion suggests that the market, although it believes in the realism of the deal, is still in doubt due to the lack of any specifics. Therefore, such a discount remains.

The important question is, how profitable is the $13 billion valuation for investors and for Sun Pharmaceutical? At the moment, the company is trading at large discounts to the sector medians and its five-year averages for the main multiples (forward P/E and EV/EBITDA). The discount on FWD EV/EBIDTA reaches almost 50% to the sector, and on FWD P/E in general almost 80%. If we assume that Organon will indeed be bought for $13 billion, then the company's EV will grow by about 16-17%, and the FWD EV/EBITDA multiplier is 6.7–6.9x. In this case, the discount to peers will be reduced to only 40-45%, but a premium to its five-year average of 3-5% will be formed. The situation will be similar with P/E.
